name: Pomegranate Cups
servings: makes 15 cups as finger food or serves 5–6 as a side dish
intro: Tabbouleh is a Levantine salad that has become a staple in so many cultures, and each variation seems to be a slightly tweaked version of the original dish. My version mirrors the authentic recipe, except for the addition of pomegranate seeds. I grew up eating tabbouleh, as it’s one of the only dishes my mum would make regularly. She taught me how to eat it – by using lettuce leaves to scoop up the salad. The addition of juicy pomegranate seeds gives it a sweet burst. I like to dish it up in Gem lettuce leaves and serve the ‘cups’ to guests. They look beautiful and taste wonderful, too.

ingredients.item: large bunch of flat leaf parsley, leaves picked and finely chopped
plum tomatoes, deseeded and finely diced
spring onions, thinly sliced right up to 7cm (3in) from the ends
sea salt and freshly ground black pepper

method: Cook the bulgar wheat as per the packet instructions, then rinse well in cold water, drain and put into a large bowl.

Mix all your chopped ingredients into the bulgur wheat, along with the cinnamon and pomegranate seeds. Drizzle with the olive oil – add just enough to ensure the mixture is well coated, but not enough to make it greasy. Now add the lemon juice and sea salt and black pepper to taste.

Take a generous tablespoon of the tabbouleh and place it in the depression of a lettuce leaf. Put the ‘cup’ on a serving platter and repeat until all the tabbouleh has been used up. Serve the cups on the platter as a salad, or on plates alongside a main dish.
